original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what caveat is documented for SNMP?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what issue was resolved in Internal srvcs: pspo Bug 267398?
original_answer: Internal srvcs: pspo (Bug ID 267398): Symptom: VXLAN tunnels go down after removing interfaces with IPv6 address that are the same as the VXLAN VTEP IP addresses. Scenario: In an EVPN-VXLAN deployment with an IPv6 tunnel, if any interface (irrespective of the VRF) that has same IP address as the tunnel source IP, it goes down, and then the tunnel interface is brought down. Workaround: Unconfigure loopback and VXLAN and re-configure them.

Workaround: Unconfigure loopback and VXLAN and re-configure them.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what issue was resolved in WebUI Bug 279019?
original_answer: WebUI (Bug ID 279019): Symptom: After uploading an invalid PEM certificate, the hpe-restd process becomes unstable, the WebUI temporarily stops responding, and all REST API calls from the WebUI fail. Scenario: If a user uploads a corrupt PEM certificate file using WebUI certificate management window, selecting the Upload button in the WebUI causes the WebUI to stop working completely. original_answer: Internal srvcs: Security PA infra (Bug ID 278954): Symptom: Clients are onboarding with an incorrect auth-priority order. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.0010, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1000, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1000, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1001, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 289568?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 289568): Symptom: The root switch initiates STP re-convergence and causes causing MAC flush and traffic loss. Scenario: This issue occurs during an upgrade process in VSX pair (which acts as non-root switch in RPVST topology). When the primary switch is down, this issue is encountered in a non-root access switch connected to this VSX-pair. Workaround: Install BPDU filter on the root-port of access switch during the software upgrade and remove the filter after the upgrade.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what issue was resolved in IGMP/MLD Bug 292078?
original_answer: IGMP/MLD (Bug ID 292078): Symptom: The event message, Event ID 2628: “IGMP/MLD internal queue limit exceeded. Needs admin intervention” is logged multiple times. Scenario: This issue is observed in VXLAN overlay networks that do not have any multicast configuration. Workaround: Enable IGMP snooping on each VLAN carried in the overlay on all switches acting as VTEPs. Alternatively, use the logging filter command to deny log messages with the Event ID 2628.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what issue was resolved in Central Bug 293314?
original_answer: Central (Bug ID 293314): Symptom: The client list is not populated in the Central client view page. Scenario: In very rare instances, after maintenance is performed on Aruba Central infrastructure, the client list in the Central client view page may fail to list the connected clients for some switches. This issue is uncommon enough that an upgrade to this release may not be warranted if the only reason for the upgrade is to avoid this potential issue. Workaround: From the switch console, enter bash via the start-shell command, and execute the command systemctl restart yang-resolverd.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what issue was resolved in VSX Sync Bug 299838?
original_answer: VSX Sync (Bug ID 299838): Symptom: The interface VLAN configuration did not synchronize on the secondary switch. Scenario: This issue is observed in a pair of switches where the user configures an interface VLAN with vsx-sync configuration on the primary VSX and then creates the same interface VLAN on the secondary switch. Workaround: When vsx-sync is enabled on the interface VLAN of the primary VSX member, it is recommended to wait for at least 15 seconds before configuring the same on the secondary switch. Alternatively, configure the interface VLAN on the secondary switch and then enable vsx-sync on the primary switch.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 291905?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 291905): Symptom: Packet drop is observed and packets are not redirected to the other VSX member if the ISL LAG is reconfigured when the local MCLAG is down. Scenario: If the local MCLAG port on a VSX member is down and the MCLAG on the other VSX member is up, then traffic should be redirected over ISL to the VSX partner. If the ISL LAG is reconfigured, then the redirection will fail, causing packets headed toward that MCLAG to be dropped. Workaround: Remove and re-configure the ISL LAG and assign port membership before setting the LAG as ISL.

reason: The answer does not directly address the issue resolved in Bug 305843. It provides a workaround instead of describing the bug and its resolution.
original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in ACL Logging Bug 305843?
original_answer: ACL Logging (Bug ID 305843): Symptom: Switch reloads unexpectedly during a firmware upgrade. Scenario: This issue occurs during an upgrade where a switch might reload while parsing an Access List (ACL) configuration. Workaround: It is recommended to follow the following procedure before the upgrade: Save the full configuration as a checkpoint using the copy running-config checkpoint <name> command. Remove all access-list configurations, save the configuration as the startup configuration using the copy running-config startup-config command. Upgrade the switch. Once the switch is upgraded, move the ACLs back to the running configuration by copying the saved checkpoint to the running configuration using the copy checkpoint <name> running-config command.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what is the workaround for ACL Logging Bug 305843?
original_answer: ACL Logging (Bug ID 305843): The documented workaround is: It is recommended to follow the following procedure before the upgrade: Save the full configuration as a checkpoint using the copy running-config checkpoint <name> command. Remove all access-list configurations, save the configuration as the startup configuration using the copy running-config startup-config command. Upgrade the switch. Once the switch is upgraded, move the ACLs back to the running configuration by copying the saved checkpoint to the running configuration using the copy checkpoint <name> running-config command.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in Supportability Bug 311012?
original_answer: Supportability (Bug ID 311012): Symptom: High storage utilization is observed in the file system and as a result, users are unable to copy the support files. Scenario: Logs from various daemons are continuously generated and stored in multiple files in the /fs/logs/boot/ directory. This leads to a high storage utilization and prevents the copying of support files. Workaround: Use the directory mode support for file collection. After upgrading the device to the fixed firmware version, it is necessary to remove the previous boot logs. Performing two system reboots will clear these files.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 298054?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 298054): Symptom: The MAC address learnt on the local VSX switch did not show up on the peer VSX switch. Scenario: This issue occurs when a member port is removed and another member port is added to the ISL lag during the same time. This issue is also observed when a physical port on the ISL lag is changed quickly. Workaround: Delete the existing ISL lag, recreate the LAG, add the ports to the LAG, and then set the LAG as ISL.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what issue was resolved in Central Bug 300693?
original_answer: Central (Bug ID 300693): Symptom: Firmware updates via Aruba Central fails with an event log, Event | 4403 | LOG_ERR | CDTR | 1 | User admin: primary image update failed via HTTPS from common.cloud.hpe.com. Scenario: This issue is observed when the switch is configured to use http-proxy and the switch's upstream DNS server does not have an entry for common.cloud.hpe.com and when DNS recursion is disabled. Workaround: Configure a static DNS entry for common.cloud.hpe.com using the ip dns host command.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1031, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1031, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, what issue was resolved in VSX Sync Bug 309500?
original_answer: VSX Sync (Bug ID 309500): Symptom: VSX-Sync is no longer syncing after a command for BGP to redistribute to OSPF with a route map is used. Scenario: This issue is seen on a pair of switches in a VSX configuration, with syncing for BGP active and when the user configures a redistribution for BGP to OSPF with a route map on the primary switch. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1060, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1060, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1060, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 336714?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 336714): Symptom: When DHCP snooping option 82 behavior needed but not supported on 9300. Scenario: DHCP client is trying to get an IP and DHCP server is configured to give IPv4 address based on the Circuit-id and Remote-Id. Workaround: Enable the dhcp-relay option 82 circuit-id port-id command to achieve DHCP Snooping option 82 behavior in DHCP-Relay itself. By enabling this command, DHCP Relay will send option 82 information similar to DHCP-Snooping.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 336714?
cleaned_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 336714): Symptom: When DHCP snooping option 82 behavior is needed but not supported on 9300. Scenario: A DHCP client attempts to get an IP address, while the DHCP server is configured to provide IPv4 addresses based on Circuit-id and Remote-Id. Workaround: Enable the dhcp-relay option 82 circuit-id port-id command to achieve DHCP Snooping option 82 behavior in DHCP-Relay itself. By enabling this command, DHCP Relay will send option 82 information similar to DHCP-Snooping.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what issue was resolved in ARP Bug 335299?
original_answer: ARP (Bug ID 335299): Symptom: Client ARP entries are not aging out on the VSX peer switch. Scenario: When an ARP entry is learned and synced to the VSX peer, followed by a client IP change at the end host, the old ARP entries do not age out on the switch. Workaround: Manually clear the ARP entries on the respective VSX peer switch.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what issue was resolved in ARP Bug 326400?
original_answer: ARP (Bug ID 326400): Symptom: Windows virtual machines detect duplicate IP addresses during Windows cluster failover. Scenario: During a Windows cluster failover, a Windows virtual machine sends an ARP probe for its own IP address to detect any duplicate IPs in the network. With ARP suppression enabled, the switch replies to the ARP probe instead of forwarding it through the VXLAN fabric. Workaround: Disable ARP suppression under the EVPN configuration.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, what issue was resolved in IGMP Bug 338204?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 338204): Symptom: An IPTV may experience a delay when changing channels. This problem may occur intermittently depending on the number of multicast entries. Scenario: The multicast entries present in the database need to be updated with the activity-status every 30 seconds. The activity-status need to be fetched from the hardware for all the multicast entries. This is taking 5-6 seconds of time in the customer network. So, the channel change, which is leading to the forwarding port change in the hardware, is pending behind the update of activity-status of multicast entries.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1090, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1090, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1090, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what issue was resolved in ACL Bug 234200?
original_answer: ACL (Bug ID 234200): Symptom: After restoring a checkpoint on the VSX primary switch that contains ACLs configured with vsx-sync, the ACL fails to apply on the VSX secondary switch and is not functional. Warning messages appear in the configuration, but no event logs are generated to indicate the ACL application failure. As a result, traffic that should be blocked by the ACL continues to pass through the secondary switch. Scenario: This issue occurs in the following scenario: 1. A VSX pair is configured. 2. ACLs are configured on the primary switch with vsx-sync enabled. 3. The ACLs are applied to interfaces or VLANs with vsx-sync enabled. 4. A checkpoint is created with this configuration. 5. The checkpoint is later restored on the primary switch. When examining the configuration on the secondary switch after checkpoint restoration, warning messages similar to the following appear: access-list ip list1 vsx-sync ! 10 deny any any any count ! access-list ip list1 user configuration does not match active configuration. ! run 'access-list TYPE NAME reset' or 'access-list all reset' to reset the ACL to match the active configuration. Workaround: Reapply the ACL on the primary switch to each interface where it is applied. This triggers proper synchronization to the secondary switch. The following is an example of proper synchronization: vlan 110 apply access-list ip list1 out vlan 120 apply access-list ip list1 out vlan 130 apply access-list ip list1 out.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 360748?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 360748): Symptom: BGP selects the wrong path, causing routing issues. Scenario: In some scenarios BGP incorrectly selects the path with the highest AS-Path. This causes incorrect paths to be propagated to the Routing Information Base (RIB) and Forwarding Information Base (FIB), potentially causing routing issues. Workaround: Lower the local preference of EVPN originated router. This causes the higher local preference route to be selected.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 359279?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 359279): Symptom: After a virtual machine is migrated (vMotion) from one data center to another in a static VXLAN environment, the VM cannot reach its gateway or external destinations, resulting in ping timeouts and connection failures. Scenario: In some scenarios, the switch can fail to program tunnel to VNI associations, leading to traffic loss. Workaround: Remove the affected VNI from the configuration on the switches in the source data center and then add the VNI to the configuration again.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 359666?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 359666): Symptom: With hpe-relay core dump legacy bootp client does not get IP. Scenario: When the DHCP relay daemon processes a legacy BOOTP client packet that lacks the option-53 field, it fails to properly validate the packet's structure before attempting to access this field, leading to a crash. This issue occurs exclusively when the smart-relay feature is enabled. Workaround: Disable the global DHCP smart-relay configuration if not required using no dhcp-smart-relay.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what issue was resolved in Multicast Bug 338204?
original_answer: Multicast (Bug ID 338204): Symptom: IPTV may experience a delay when changing channels. This problem may occur intermittently depending on the number of multicast entries. Scenario: The multicast entries present in the database need to be updated with the activity-status every 30 seconds. The activity-status need to be fetched from the hardware for all multicast entries. This is taking 5-6 seconds of time and delays the channel change, which leads to the forwarding port change in the hardware. Workaround: The multicast activity-status update is improved to fetch the status from all of the LCs at the same time instead of fetching it one-by-one from each of the LCs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what issue was resolved in Multicast Bug 342612?
original_answer: Multicast (Bug ID 342612): Symptom: When switching between IPTV channels, there is an intermittent delay of 2-6 seconds before the new video stream is displayed to the end user. Scenario: The system experiences a small delay in the program of a new multicast flow after receiving the IGMP report. During this time, critical initial video packets containing SPS (Sequence Parameter Set) and PPS (Picture Parameter Set) information may be dropped. These packets are essential for the decoder to properly display the H.264 video stream. Workaround: Configure static IGMP groups for the multicast streams using static igmp join <multicast-group-address> interface <decoder-port>. This ensures the hardware is pre-programmed with the multicast flows, eliminating the join latency.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what issue was resolved in REST Bug 353178?
original_answer: REST (Bug ID 353178): Symptom: When uploading firmware using the Web UI or REST API, the system displays the following error message despite the firmware being successfully transferred to the device: There was an error uploading new firmware. Firmware image is invalid. Please enter a valid firmware image. Scenario: When logging console is enabled in the switch configuration and a user attempts to upload firmware through the Web UI or REST API, the upload process is completed, but an HTTP 500 error is generated. Workaround: Until upgrading to the fixed version, use one of the following methods: 1. Disable console logging by removing logging console from the configuration. Then attempt the firmware upload using the Web UI or REST API again. 2. Use TFTP for firmware upload by uploading the firmware using TFTP or SFTP instead of the Web UI or REST API. Example command: copy tftp/sftp://[server-ip]/[firmware-file] primary vrf mgmt.

Workaround: Until upgrading to the fixed version, disable console logging by removing 'logging console' from the configuration and attempt the firmware upload again using the Web UI or REST API. Alternatively, use TFTP for firmware upload by uploading the firmware using TFTP or SFTP instead of the Web UI or REST API. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1101, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1110, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1110, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1110, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 360748?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 360748): Symptom: BGP selects the wrong path, causing routing issues. Scenario: In some scenarios BGP incorrectly selects the path with the highest AS-Path. This causes incorrect paths to be propagated to the Routing Information Base (RIB) and Forwarding Information Base (FIB), potentially causing routing issues. Workaround: Lower the local preference of EVPN originated router. The higher local preference route is selected.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1110, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1120, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1120, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1120, what issue was resolved in Logging Bug 358847?
original_answer: Logging (Bug ID 358847): Symptom: A VSF stack member switch unexpectedly reboots due to the ops-switchd process crashing with a signal:6 error. Scenario: In some scenarios with excessive ACL event logging, a crash may occur due to a failure in the system when processing a high volume of ACL log entries. Workaround: Minimize the use of the log keyword in ACL entries, especially on high-traffic rules. Consider using ACL counters instead of logging for monitoring traffic patterns.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1120, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 373232?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 373232): Symptom: Multicast traffic loss may occur on interfaces with PIM-Sparse Mode (PIM-SM) enabled in overlay networks. Affected multicast flows fail to replicate across VXLAN tunnels, resulting in service disruption. Scenario: In VSX deployments, multicast replication may fail during system boot or reload if the associated L3 interfaces for overlay VLANs are not fully initialized. This is more likely in configurations involving MCLAGs and delayed VSX synchronization, where multicast processing begins before the L3 interface is ready. Workaround: Toggle the pim-sparse configuration on the impacted interfaces.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1130, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1130, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1130, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 373232?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 373232): Symptom: Multicast traffic loss may occur on interfaces with PIM-Sparse Mode (PIM-SM) enabled in overlay networks. Affected multicast flows fail to replicate across VXLAN tunnels, resulting in service disruption. Scenario: In VSX deployments, multicast replication may fail during system boot or reload if the associated L3 interfaces for overlay VLANs are not fully initialized. This is more likely in configurations involving MCLAGs and delayed VSX synchronization, where multicast processing begins before the L3 interface is ready. Workaround: Toggle the pim-sparse configuration on the impacted interfaces.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1130, what issue was resolved in EVPN Bug 364576?
original_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 364576): Symptom: Missing ARP entries showing as "incomplete" in the ARP table, causing connectivity issues in remote VTEP. Scenario: This issue may occur in an EVPN VXLAN environment with VSX pairs when a VIP (Virtual IP) moves from one node to another node, MAC address aging occurs or after executing "clear mac-address" on the switch. Workaround: A temporary workaround is to add a static ARP entry for the affected IP addresses.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1140, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1140, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1150, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1150, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1150, what issue was resolved in Container Manager Bug 366132?
original_answer: Container Manager (Bug ID 366132): Symptom: Container statuses are not updated after deleting and recreating the containers. Scenario: This issue can occur with these following time sensitive steps: 1. Container A is created, Container Manager is processing the creation of said container. 2. A delete request for container A is sent using CLI or REST. 3. A request to create container A is sent. 4. When checking for statuses using REST or show container, the container is marked as down. Workaround: Where possible, avoid deleting and recreating the same container while it is being created, allow it to reach the operational state. If the issue is encountered, delete the container using REST or CLI and restart the switch to release the occupied container resources.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1150, what issue was resolved in IGMP Bug 400918?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 400918): Symptom: The replication group of MCAST entry points to overlay-egress-obj, which does not exist in the hardware. The replication group points to the right net-vp. As a result, the VXLAN encapsulated packet containing the MCAST inner packet goes out with outer header SA and DST MAC as all zeros. Scenario: This issue occurs when the underlay nexthop changes towards the VTEP, which is connected to the client. The nexthop change can happen in the following cases: spine reboot, link flap, and interface MTU change.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1160, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1160, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1160, what known issue is documented for PBR Bug 277126?
original_answer: PBR (Bug ID 277126): Symptom: Classifier or PBR Policy is not enforced for traffic coming via ISL link. Scenario: In a VSX+ VRRP topology, if a policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting the classifier policy enforced. Workaround: To prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL, use active gateway instead of VRRP. This provides better active-active redundancy and packets reaching either VSX switch process the packet destined to active gateway.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1161, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1161, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1170, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1170, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1170, what issue was resolved in MAC Tables Bug 400513?
original_answer: MAC Tables (Bug ID 400513): Symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. Scenario: This issue can occur resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move from one VSX device to the other, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Using the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

cleaned_answer: MAC Tables (Bug ID 400513): Symptom: MAC addresses are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For example, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. Scenario: This issue can occur when resolving a network loop, causing MACs to quickly move between VSX devices, especially when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Use the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.13.1170, what issue was resolved in PBR Bug 407418?
original_answer: PBR (Bug ID 407418): Symptom: Classifier Policy is not enforced for traffic coming from an ISL link. Scenario: For a topology with VSX+ VRRP, if the policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting classifier policy enforced. Even though the policy action is PBR redirect, those packets are not getting redirected. Workaround: Two workarounds are possible to prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL. 1. Use active gateway to give better active active redundancy. Packets reaching either VSX device process the packet destined to active gateway. 2. Turn off the interface connection from VRRP secondary device to down switch. This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

This cycle of connecting and disconnecting continues until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To prevent this unnecessary reconnection, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what issue was resolved in IGMP Bug 215677?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 215677): Symptom: IGMP/MLD configuration changes do not take effect when the hpe-mgmdd process is flooded with IGMP/MLD control packets. Scenario: In a situation where IGMP or MLD control packets (Joins or Leaves) are part of a loop, or if misbehaving clients send continuous streams of IGMP/MLD control packets, the hpe-mgmdd daemon's packet queue can become continuously full. Workaround: Restart the MGMD daemon using the command systemctl restart hpe-mgmdd.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what issue was resolved in Thermal Manager Bug 288499?
original_answer: Thermal Manager (Bug ID 288499): Symptom: A thermal shutdown is triggered by certain sensors (Front-of-MAC, Side-of-MAC, Bottom) crossing a critical temperature threshold. Additionally, A user may observe a thermal shutdown if a specific sensor (FPGA) crosses a critical temperature threshold. Scenario: Temperature sensors have predetermined thresholds that are used to ensure safe thermal operation, which may include a thermal shutdown of the switch. AOS-CX 10.14.0001 updates the thresholds for various sensors to extend their safe operating range, and also lowers the threshold for one sensor to ensure a safe operating range. It is not expected that users would encounter these scenarios in normal operating environments. Workaround: Ensure that there is sufficient cooling and airflow provided to the switch.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what issue was resolved in the Thermal Manager for Bug ID 288499?
cleaned_answer: Thermal Manager (Bug ID 288499): Symptom: Certain sensors (Front-of-MAC, Side-of-MAC, Bottom) trigger a thermal shutdown when crossing critical temperature thresholds. A specific sensor (FPGA) can also cause a thermal shutdown if it crosses its critical threshold. Scenario: Temperature sensors have predefined thresholds to ensure safe thermal operation, which may include a thermal shutdown of the switch. AOS-CX 10.14.0001 updates the thresholds for various sensors to extend their safe operating range and lowers one sensor's threshold for safety. Normal users should not encounter these scenarios. Workaround: Ensure adequate cooling and airflow for the switch.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 291742?

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what issue was resolved in VSX-Sync Bug 299838?
original_answer: VSX-Sync (Bug ID 299838): Symptom: An Interface VLAN configuration is not getting synced to secondary switch. Scenario: This issue can occur n a pair of switches in VSX configuration, when the user creates an Interface VLAN with VSX-Sync configuration in primary switch, then the user creates the same Interface VLAN on secondary, but the configuration is not synced. Workaround: Once VSX-Sync has been enabled on primary Interface VLAN, wait ~15 seconds before creating the Interface VLAN on the secondary interface. Another workaround is to first create the Interface VLAN on the secondary and then enable VSX-Sync on the primary Interface VLAN. (There is no need to wait in this second workaround).

Workaround: Once VSX-Sync has been enabled on the primary Interface VLAN, wait ~15 seconds before creating the Interface VLAN on the secondary switch. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 299851?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 299851): Symptom: The VSX software upgrade software version is not properly validated when upgrading using TFTP. Scenario: When upgrading using TFTP, it is possible to load mismatched software versions, resulting in a VSX upgrade failure and a VSX software mismatch between the primary and secondary VSX nodes. Workaround: Confirm that software image versions are compatible. Software upgrades using boot banks rather than TFTP will perform a proper version check before completing the upgrade.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what issue was resolved in Internal srvcs: Security PA infra Bug 301734?
original_answer: Internal srvcs: Security PA infra (Bug ID 301734): Symptom: A role assigned by CoA gets overridden by low-priority methods, such as a RADIUS assigned role. Scenario: This issue occurs on a switch with the default auth-priority on port ifyou configure both mac-auth and dot1x and assign a role from RADIUS on successful mac-auth authentication and concurrent on boarding. While dot1x is in authenticating state, if you assign a different role RADIUS using CoA, you can observe that role assigned by CoA gets overridden by the RADIUS-assigned role (during mac-auth success). Workaround: Remove concurrent onboarding.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0001, what issue was resolved in Internal srvcs: Security PA infra Bug 301734?
cleaned_answer: Internal srvcs: Security PA infra (Bug ID 301734): Symptom: A role assigned by CoA gets overridden by low-priority methods, such as a RADIUS assigned role. Scenario: This issue occurs on a switch with the default auth-priority on port if you configure both mac-auth and dot1x and assign a role from RADIUS on successful mac-auth authentication and concurrent onboarding. While dot1x is in authenticating state, if you assign a different role via RADIUS using CoA, you can observe that the role assigned by CoA gets overridden by the RADIUS-assigned role (during mac-auth success). Workaround: Remove concurrent onboarding.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0006, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0006, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will be disconnected. This cycle of connecting and disconnecting continues until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To prevent this unnecessary reconnection, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0006, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.0007, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will be disconnected. To prevent this unnecessary reconnection, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1000, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1000, what known issue is documented for PTP Bug 301728?
original_answer: PTP (Bug ID 301728): Symptom: With the switch is configured with PTP protocol on multiple ports (both physical and lag) and user were to remove or add lag port to a clock source port, the sink port could flap. Scenario: In most cases, any PTP-related configuration changes will cause PTP services in the system to restart, which affects all the ports. Adding or removing a LAG port, can also cause PTP services to restart. There is a potential risk of of data traffic being miss-classified as PTP protocol packets for a very short window (milliseconds) and with enough of this traffic type, the PTP service will encounter the condition as if there were no sync packets from the grand source device. As a result, the PTP sink port will flap to re-establish the PTP protocol. However, even with the PTP sink port flap, the system can converge quickly and should not cause high offsets to downstream PTP clients. Workaround: Best practice is to implement PTP configuration changes during network downtime or a maintenance window.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1000, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.14.1000, what known issue is documented for PTP Bug 301728?
cleaned_answer: PTP (Bug ID 301728): Symptom: When the switch is configured with PTP protocol on multiple ports (both physical and lag) and a user removes or adds a lag port to a clock source port, the sink port could flap. Scenario: Any PTP-related configuration changes typically cause PTP services in the system to restart, affecting all ports. Adding or removing a LAG port can also cause PTP services to restart. There is a risk of data traffic being misclassified as PTP protocol packets for a short period (milliseconds), which could lead the PTP service to treat the absence of sync packets from the grand source device as an issue, causing the PTP sink port to flap. However, even with the PTP sink port flapping, the system should converge quickly and not cause high offsets in downstream PTP clients. Workaround: Best practice is to implement PTP configuration changes during network downtime or a maintenance window.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1000, what issue was resolved in VSX-Sync Bug 309500?
original_answer: VSX-Sync (Bug ID 309500): Symptom: VSX sync is no longer syncing after a configuring BGP to redistribute to OSPF with a route map. Scenario: This issue can occur on a pair of switches in a VSX configuration that are syncing for BGP when a user configures redistribution for BGP to OSPF with a route map on the primary switch. VSX sync will not sync the redistribution configuration or any other new configuration. Workaround: Disable BGP syncing or remove the redistribution for BGP to OSPF with a route map.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1001, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1001, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1001, what known issue is documented for PTP Bug 301728?
original_answer: PTP (Bug ID 301728): Symptom: With the switch is configured with PTP protocol on multiple ports (both physical and lag) and user were to remove or add lag port to a clock source port, the sink port could flap. Scenario: In most cases, any PTP-related configuration changes will cause PTP services in the system to restart, which affects all the ports. Adding or removing a LAG port, can also cause PTP services to restart. There is a potential risk of of data traffic being miss-classified as PTP protocol packets for a very short window (milliseconds) and with enough of this traffic type, the PTP service will encounter the condition as if there were no sync packets from the grand source device. As a result, the PTP sink port will flap to re-establish the PTP protocol. However, even with the PTP sink port flap, the system can converge quickly and should not cause high offsets to downstream PTP clients. Workaround: Best practice is to implement PTP configuration changes during network downtime or a maintenance window.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1001, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.14.1001, what known issue is documented for PTP Bug 301728?
cleaned_answer: PTP (Bug ID 301728): Symptom: With the switch configured with PTP protocol on multiple ports (both physical and lag) and a user removing or adding a LAG port to a clock source port, the sink port could flap. Scenario: Any PTP-related configuration changes will cause PTP services in the system to restart, affecting all ports. Adding or removing a LAG port can also cause PTP services to restart. There is a potential risk of data traffic being misclassified as PTP protocol packets for a very short window (milliseconds) and with enough of this traffic type, the PTP service will encounter the condition as if there were no sync packets from the grand source device. As a result, the PTP sink port will flap to re-establish the PTP protocol. However, even with the PTP sink port flap, the system can converge quickly and should not cause high offsets to downstream PTP clients. Workaround: Best practice is to implement PTP configuration changes during network downtime or a maintenance window.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1010, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1010, what issue was resolved in IGMP Bug 295438?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 295438): Symptom: IGMP/MLD control packets were not processed and the querier information was not learnt. Scenario: This issue is observed in a VXLAN setup without IGMP/MLD configurations where multicast control packets arrive continuously through the VLAN which does not have IGMP/MLD configuration. Workaround: Enable IGMP/MLD snooping on all the VLANs where multicast clients send IGMP/MLD control packets.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1010, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1010, what known issue is documented for PTP Bug 301728?
original_answer: PTP (Bug ID 301728): Symptom: With the switch is configured with PTP protocol on multiple ports (both physical and lag) and user were to remove or add lag port to a clock source port, the sink port could flap. Scenario: In most cases, any PTP-related configuration changes will cause PTP services in the system to restart, which affects all the ports. Adding or removing a LAG port, can also cause PTP services to restart. There is a potential risk of of data traffic being miss-classified as PTP protocol packets for a very short window (milliseconds) and with enough of this traffic type, the PTP service will encounter the condition as if there were no sync packets from the grand source device. As a result, the PTP sink port will flap to re-establish the PTP protocol. However, even with the PTP sink port flap, the system can converge quickly and should not cause high offsets to downstream PTP clients. Workaround: Best practice is to implement PTP configuration changes during network downtime or a maintenance window.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1010, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.14.1010, what known issue is documented for PTP Bug 301728?
cleaned_answer: PTP (Bug ID 301728): Symptom: With the switch configured with PTP protocol on multiple ports (both physical and lag) and a user removing or adding a LAG port to a clock source port, the sink port could flap. Scenario: Any PTP-related configuration changes can cause PTP services in the system to restart, affecting all ports. Adding or removing a LAG port can also cause PTP services to restart. There is a potential risk of data traffic being misclassified as PTP protocol packets for a very short window (milliseconds) and with enough of this traffic type, the PTP service will encounter the condition as if there were no sync packets from the grand source device. As a result, the PTP sink port will flap to re-establish the PTP protocol. However, even with the PTP sink port flap, the system can converge quickly and should not cause high offsets to downstream PTP clients. Workaround: Best practice is to implement PTP configuration changes during network downtime or a maintenance window.
