original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will be disconnected. This cycle of connecting and disconnecting continues until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To prevent this unnecessary reconnection, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1020, what caveat is documented for Certificates?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1020, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1020, what known issue is documented for PTP Bug 301728?
original_answer: PTP (Bug ID 301728): Symptom: With the switch is configured with PTP protocol on multiple ports (both physical and lag) and user were to remove or add lag port to a clock source port, the sink port could flap. Scenario: In most cases, any PTP-related configuration changes will cause PTP services in the system to restart, which affects all the ports. Adding or removing a LAG port, can also cause PTP services to restart. There is a potential risk of of data traffic being miss-classified as PTP protocol packets for a very short window (milliseconds) and with enough of this traffic type, the PTP service will encounter the condition as if there were no sync packets from the grand source device. As a result, the PTP sink port will flap to re-establish the PTP protocol. However, even with the PTP sink port flap, the system can converge quickly and should not cause high offsets to downstream PTP clients. Workaround: Best practice is to implement PTP configuration changes during network downtime or a maintenance window.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1020, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.14.1020, what known issue is documented for PTP Bug 301728?
cleaned_answer: PTP (Bug ID 301728): Symptom: When the switch is configured with PTP protocol on multiple ports (both physical and lag) and a user removes or adds a lag port to a clock source port, the sink port could flap. Scenario: Any PTP-related configuration changes typically cause PTP services in the system to restart, affecting all ports. Adding or removing a LAG port can also cause PTP services to restart. There is a risk of data traffic being misclassified as PTP protocol packets for a short period (milliseconds), which could lead the PTP service to treat the absence of sync packets from the grand source device as an issue, causing the PTP sink port to flap. However, even with the PTP sink port flapping, the system should converge quickly and not cause high offsets in downstream PTP clients. Workaround: Best practice is to implement PTP configuration changes during network downtime or a maintenance window.

There is a potential risk of of data traffic being miss-classified as PTP protocol packets for a very short window (milliseconds) and with enough of this traffic type, the PTP service will encounter the condition as if there were no sync packets from the grand source device. As a result, the PTP sink port will flap to re-establish the PTP protocol. However, even with the PTP sink port flap, the system can converge quickly and should not cause high offsets to downstream PTP clients..

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1030, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1030, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 336714?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 336714): Symptom: When DHCP snooping option 82 behavior needed but not supported on 9300. Scenario: DHCP client is trying to get an IP and DHCP server is configured to give IPv4 address based on the Circuit-id and Remote-Id. Workaround: Enable the dhcp-relay option 82 circuit-id port-id command to achieve DHCP Snooping option 82 behavior in DHCP-Relay itself. By enabling this command, DHCP Relay will send option 82 information similar to DHCP-Snooping.

Scenario: A DHCP client attempts to get an IP address, while the DHCP server is configured to provide IPv4 addresses based on Circuit-id and Remote-Id. Workaround: Enable the dhcp-relay option 82 circuit-id port-id command to achieve DHCP Snooping option 82 behavior in DHCP-Relay itself. By enabling this command, DHCP Relay will send option 82 information similar to DHCP-Snooping.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1030, what issue was resolved in ARP Bug 335299?
original_answer: ARP (Bug ID 335299): Symptom: Client ARP entries are not aging out on the VSX peer switch. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1030, what issue was resolved in ARP Bug 326400?
original_answer: ARP (Bug ID 326400): Symptom: Windows virtual machines detect duplicate IP addresses during Windows cluster failover. Scenario: During a Windows cluster failover, a Windows virtual machine sends an ARP probe for its own IP address to detect any duplicate IPs in the network. With ARP suppression enabled, the switch replies to the ARP probe instead of forwarding it through the VXLAN fabric. Workaround: Disable ARP suppression under the EVPN configuration.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1030, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will be disconnected. This cycle of connecting and disconnecting continues until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To prevent this unnecessary reconnection, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1040, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. As the implementation is software-based, the stop times for MACsec secure channel and secure associations are only updated when they are deleted and therefore never updated in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - The implementation uses a software-based approach to track start and stop times for secure channels and associations. Consequently, the stop times are only updated when the secure channel or association is deleted, and thus are not reflected in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 341465): Symptom: Switch is unable to sync with NTP server even though NTP server is reachable and responding to the client. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1040, what issue was resolved in IGMP Bug 338204?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 338204): Symptom: An IPTV may experience a delay when changing channels. This problem may occur intermittently depending on the number of multicast entries. Scenario: The multicast entries present in the database need to be updated with the activity-status every 30 seconds. The activity-status need to be fetched from the hardware for all the multicast entries. This is taking 5-6 seconds of time in the customer network. So, the channel change, which is leading to the forwarding port change in the hardware, is pending behind the update of activity-status of multicast entries.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1040, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1050, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1050, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will be disconnected. This cycle of connecting and disconnecting continues until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To prevent this unnecessary reconnection, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1050, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. As the implementation is software-based, the stop times for MACsec secure channel and secure associations are only updated when they are deleted and therefore never updated in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1050, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - The implementation uses a software-based approach to track start and stop times for secure channels and associations. Consequently, the stop times are only updated when the secure channel or association is deleted, and thus are not reflected in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1050, what issue was resolved in MSTP Bug 346938?
original_answer: MSTP (Bug ID 346938): Symptom: Intermittent MSTP/RPVST starvation messages observed coming from source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00. Scenario: When 3rd party legacy devices send packets with source mac address 00:00:00:00:00:00 to CX devices intermittent BPDU starvation might be observed. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1050, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will be disconnected. This cycle of connecting and disconnecting continues until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To prevent this unnecessary reconnection, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what issue was resolved in ACL Bug 234200?
original_answer: ACL (Bug ID 234200): Symptom: After restoring a checkpoint on the VSX primary switch that contains ACLs configured with vsx-sync, the ACL fails to apply on the VSX secondary switch and is not functional. Warning messages appear in the configuration, but no event logs are generated to indicate the ACL application failure. As a result, traffic that should be blocked by the ACL continues to pass through the secondary switch. Scenario: This issue occurs in the following scenario: 1. A VSX pair is configured. 2. ACLs are configured on the primary switch with vsx-sync enabled. 3. The ACLs are applied to interfaces or VLANs with vsx-sync enabled. 4. A checkpoint is created with this configuration. 5. The checkpoint is later restored on the primary switch. When examining the configuration on the secondary switch after checkpoint restoration, warning messages similar to the following appear: access-list ip list1 vsx-sync ! 10 deny any any any count ! access-list ip list1 user configuration does not match active configuration. ! run 'access-list TYPE NAME reset' or 'access-list all reset' to reset the ACL to match the active configuration. Workaround: Reapply the ACL on the primary switch to each interface where it is applied. This triggers proper synchronization to the secondary switch. The following is an example of proper synchronization: vlan 110 apply access-list ip list1 out vlan 120 apply access-list ip list1 out vlan 130 apply access-list ip list1 out.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 359279?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 359279): Symptom: After a virtual machine is migrated (vMotion) from one data center to another in a static VXLAN environment, the VM cannot reach its gateway or external destinations, resulting in ping timeouts and connection failures. Scenario: In some scenarios, the switch can fail to program tunnel to VNI associations, leading to traffic loss. Workaround: Remove the affected VNI from the configuration on the switches in the source data center and then add the VNI to the configuration again.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 359666?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 359666): Symptom: With hpe-relay core dump legacy bootp client does not get IP. Scenario: When the DHCP relay daemon processes a legacy BOOTP client packet that lacks the option-53 field, it fails to properly validate the packet's structure before attempting to access this field, leading to a crash. This issue occurs exclusively when the smart-relay feature is enabled. Workaround: Disable the global DHCP smart-relay configuration if not required using no dhcp-smart-relay.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what issue was resolved in Multicast Bug 342612?
original_answer: Multicast (Bug ID 342612): Symptom: When switching between IPTV channels, there is an intermittent delay of 2-6 seconds before the new video stream is displayed to the end user. Scenario: The system experiences a small delay in the program of a new multicast flow after receiving the IGMP report. During this time, critical initial video packets containing SPS (Sequence Parameter Set) and PPS (Picture Parameter Set) information may be dropped. These packets are essential for the decoder to properly display the H.264 video stream. Workaround: Configure static IGMP groups for the multicast streams using static igmp join <multicast-group-address> interface <decoder-port>. This ensures the hardware is pre-programmed with the multicast flows, eliminating the join latency.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what issue was resolved in REST Bug 353178?
original_answer: REST (Bug ID 353178): Symptom: When uploading firmware using the Web UI or REST API, the system displays the following error message despite the firmware being successfully transferred to the device: There was an error uploading new firmware. Firmware image is invalid. Please enter a valid firmware image. Scenario: When logging console is enabled in the switch configuration and a user attempts to upload firmware through the Web UI or REST API, the upload process is completed, but an HTTP 500 error is generated. Workaround: Until upgrading to the fixed version, use one of the following methods: 1. Disable console logging by removing logging console from the configuration. Then attempt the firmware upload using the Web UI or REST API again. 2. Use TFTP for firmware upload by uploading the firmware using TFTP or SFTP instead of the Web UI or REST API. Example command: copy tftp/sftp://[server-ip]/[firmware-file] primary vrf mgmt.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1060, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1061, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1061, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will be disconnected. This cycle of connecting and disconnecting continues until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To prevent this unnecessary reconnection, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1061, what caveat is documented for Certificates?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1061, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1061, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - The implementation uses a software-based approach to track start and stop times for secure channels and associations. Consequently, the stop times are only updated when the secure channel or association is deleted, and thus are not reflected in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1061, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14.1061, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
cleaned_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what caveat is documented for SNMP?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what issue was resolved in Activate Bug 317632?
original_answer: Activate (Bug ID 317632): Symptom: The switch contacts the Google Public DNS server: 8.8.8.8. Scenario: This issue can occur if there is no user configuration for DNS server nor DNS hosts and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured. The switch uses the Google DNS server 8.8.8.8 by default. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what issue was resolved in Active Gateway Bug 319555?
original_answer: Active Gateway (Bug ID 319555): Symptom: Configuring the same MAC address for both VSX system-mac and active-gateway MAC can cause internal potential network issues. Scenario: Configuring the same MAC address for VSX system-mac and active-gateway MAC leads to conflicts. The system-mac VALUE, VALUE-1, and VALUE+1 are used for VSX internal processes, particularly in relation to spanning-tree. Any MAC address collision with system-mac may have adverse side effects. Workaround: Ensure that the active-gateway MAC address is different from the VSX system-mac address. Take care to avoid reusing the system-mac address for the active-gateway.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what issue was resolved in EVPN/BGP Bug 307091?
original_answer: EVPN/BGP (Bug ID 307091): Symptom: The EVPN route selection process may cause an suboptimal path selected. Scenario: During the EVPN best path selection, the IGP metric to reach the nexthop of the EVPN route may not be taken into the consideration. Workaround: Other BGP attributes (like Local preference or weight, etc.) need to be altered for the routes to select the desired path. However this is may not be feasible in all the routing policy/scenarios for the enterprise.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what issue was resolved in Spanning Tree Bug 329044?
original_answer: Spanning Tree (Bug ID 329044): Symptom: An RPVST daemon daemon restart occurred. Scenario: This issue can occur when connecting an STP-aware switch to a port where spanning-tree guard is configured. Ensure that the peer switch has an RPVST VLAN that is not configured on the switch. Workaround: Refrain from connecting STP-aware switches to these ports. Alternatively, temporarily remove the BPDU-guard configuration to prevent further daemon restarts.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 292867?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 292867): Symptom: A VSX software upgrade can take more than 20 minutes to complete. Scenario: This issue can occur during a VSX software upgrade on a VXLAN deployment without IGMP/MLD running on a few VLANs associated with a VNI, when multicast control packets are coming in continuously through the VLAN. Workaround: Enable IGMP/MLD snooping on all the VLANs where multicast clients are sending IGMP/MLD control packets.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 410569?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 410569): Symptom: Interface Vxlan configuration is disabled on 9300 platform, for spine & DC-Optimized-Spine profiles from 10.15 onwards. Scenario: If Spine profile with VxLAN configs is being used before 10.15 release, then they must change the profile to Leaf before upgrading to 10.15 or higher CX versions to avoid config loss / traffic outage. Workaround: Change to Leaf profile and perform switch reboot - before upgrading to 10.15 or higher CX versions.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what known issue is documented for ACLs Bug 305596?
original_answer: ACLs (Bug ID 305596): Symptom: When an egress ACL is applied on an egress port, traffic originating from the CPU and egressing that interface will not be matched on by the ACL. Scenario: This issue can occur when a user is sending traffic from the switch CPU and that traffic is egressing an interface with an egress ACL. Workaround: Do not send traffic from the CPU out of that interface as it will not be blocked.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes, no traffic loss will be seen. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
cleaned_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes with no traffic loss. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration use more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to exceed the max of 256 temporarily. The scenario depends on the timing and ordering of route events during that period. If this max is hit, routes will be programmed as single and will not have protection until a new event converts them back to protected routes. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0001, what known issue is documented for RADIUS Port-Access Bug 320342?
original_answer: RADIUS Port-Access (Bug ID 320342): Symptom: The RADIUS server hostname will get updated to the renewed IP addresses found in DNS server. Scenario: The problem is seen when the host addresses in DNS servers get updated against the hostnames, but the Switch fails to refresh its cache with the renewed address. Workaround: The default refresh timer configured in the switch is 300 seconds. A network administrator can configure a custom value according to their needs after which the switch would automatically update the host addresses of the RADIUS server in its cache.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what issue was resolved in Active Gateway Bug 319555?
original_answer: Active Gateway (Bug ID 319555): Symptom: Configuring the same MAC address for both VSX system-mac and active-gateway MAC can cause internal potential network issues. Scenario: Configuring the same MAC address for VSX system-mac and active-gateway MAC leads to conflicts. The system-mac VALUE, VALUE-1, and VALUE+1 are used for VSX internal processes, particularly in relation to spanning-tree. Any MAC address collision with system-mac may have adverse side effects. Workaround: Ensure that the active-gateway MAC address is different from the VSX system-mac address. Take care to avoid reusing the system-mac address for the active-gateway.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what issue was resolved in EVPN/BGP Bug 307091?
original_answer: EVPN/BGP (Bug ID 307091): Symptom: The EVPN route selection process may cause an suboptimal path selected. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what issue was resolved in Spanning Tree Bug 329044?
original_answer: Spanning Tree (Bug ID 329044): Symptom: An RPVST daemon daemon restart occurred. Scenario: This issue can occur when connecting an STP-aware switch to a port where spanning-tree guard is configured. Ensure that the peer switch has an RPVST VLAN that is not configured on the switch. Workaround: Refrain from connecting STP-aware switches to these ports. Alternatively, temporarily remove the BPDU-guard configuration to prevent further daemon restarts.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 292867?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 292867): Symptom: A VSX software upgrade can take more than 20 minutes to complete. Scenario: This issue can occur during a VSX software upgrade on a VXLAN deployment without IGMP/MLD running on a few VLANs associated with a VNI, when multicast control packets are coming in continuously through the VLAN. Workaround: Enable IGMP/MLD snooping on all the VLANs where multicast clients are sending IGMP/MLD control packets.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 410569?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 410569): Symptom: Interface Vxlan configuration is disabled on 9300 platform, for spine & DC-Optimized-Spine profiles from 10.15 onwards. Scenario: If Spine profile with VxLAN configs is being used before 10.15 release, then they must change the profile to Leaf before upgrading to 10.15 or higher CX versions to avoid config loss / traffic outage. Workaround: Change to Leaf profile and perform switch reboot - before upgrading to 10.15 or higher CX versions.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what known issue is documented for ACLs Bug 305596?
original_answer: ACLs (Bug ID 305596): Symptom: When an egress ACL is applied on an egress port, traffic originating from the CPU and egressing that interface will not be matched on by the ACL. Scenario: This issue can occur when a user is sending traffic from the switch CPU and that traffic is egressing an interface with an egress ACL. Workaround: Do not send traffic from the CPU out of that interface as it will not be blocked.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes, no traffic loss will be seen. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.0005, what known issue is documented for RADIUS Port-Access Bug 320342?
original_answer: RADIUS Port-Access (Bug ID 320342): Symptom: The RADIUS server hostname will get updated to the renewed IP addresses found in DNS server. Scenario: The problem is seen when the host addresses in DNS servers get updated against the hostnames, but the Switch fails to refresh its cache with the renewed address. Workaround: The default refresh timer configured in the switch is 300 seconds. A network administrator can configure a custom value according to their needs after which the switch would automatically update the host addresses of the RADIUS server in its cache.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes, no traffic loss will be seen. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1005, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?

Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programmed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in Mirroring Bug 342437?
original_answer: Mirroring (Bug ID 342437): Symptom: Packets near MTU limits in size may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror. Scenario: When a client configures a mirror where the destination interface has the same MTU size, mirrored packets that are close to max MTU are incorrectly dropped and not mirrored. This applies to remote/tunnel mirrors as well. Workaround: Configuring mirror destination ports as routing port and set the IP MTU size. For a VLAN interface, set the IP MTU on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the IP MTU would be 1518. For a tunneled scenario, the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in Mirroring Bug 342437?
cleaned_answer: Mirroring (Bug ID 342437): Symptom: Packets near MTU limits in size may not be present in a port or tunnel/remote based mirror. Scenario: When a client configures a mirror where the destination interface has the same MTU size, mirrored packets that are close to max MTU are incorrectly dropped and not mirrored. This applies to remote/tunnel mirrors as well. Workaround: Configuring mirror destination ports as routing port and set the IP MTU size. For a VLAN interface, set the IP MTU on the VLAN SVI. IP MTU must be set to accommodate the ethernet overhead +tag. For the default MTU, 1500, the IP MTU would be 1518. For a tunneled scenario, the MTU must also account for the tunnel header.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 339717?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 339717): Symptom: ops-switchd crash while collecting VXLAN diagdump or collecting support files all. Scenario: In some VXLAN scenarios with multiple LAGs and multiple overlay VLAN on those lags. After device reboot, the LAG interface could show incorrect VXLAN Programming. Workaround: Ensure LAG interfaces are up before configuring the VLANs for the lag.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in SNMP Bug 335246?
original_answer: SNMP (Bug ID 335246): Symptom: Delay of ~30s observed when updating VLAN access config via snmpset and verifying via snmpget immediately after. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what issue was resolved in ACL Bug 234200?
original_answer: ACL (Bug ID 234200): Symptom: ACL fails to apply only the VSX secondary switch. ACL warning logs display the ! access-list ip <name> user configuration does not match active configuration. ! error. Scenario: In some scenarios when a config checkpoint restore or classifier list-update is performed in a VSX member, the ACL config will not be applied to the VSX secondary if the ACL config has vsx-sync is enabled. Workaround: Remove the ACL configuration and then re-apply the ACL.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes, no traffic loss will be seen. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1010, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
cleaned_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes with no traffic loss. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration use more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to exceed the max of 256 temporarily. The scenario depends on the timing and ordering of route events during that period. If this max is hit, routes will be programmed as single and won't have protection until a new event converts them back to protected routes. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in ACL Bug 234200?
original_answer: ACL (Bug ID 234200): Symptom: After restoring a checkpoint on the VSX primary switch that contains ACLs configured with vsx-sync, the ACL fails to apply on the VSX secondary switch and is not functional. Warning messages appear in the configuration, but no event logs are generated to indicate the ACL application failure. As a result, traffic that should be blocked by the ACL continues to pass through the secondary switch. Scenario: This issue occurs in the following scenario: 1. A VSX pair is configured. 2. ACLs are configured on the primary switch with vsx-sync enabled. 3. The ACLs are applied to interfaces or VLANs with vsx-sync enabled. 4. A checkpoint is created with this configuration. 5. The checkpoint is later restored on the primary switch. When examining the configuration on the secondary switch after checkpoint restoration, warning messages similar to the following appear: access-list ip list1 vsx-sync ! 10 deny any any any count ! access-list ip list1 user configuration does not match active configuration. ! run 'access-list TYPE NAME reset' or 'access-list all reset' to reset the ACL to match the active configuration. Workaround: Reapply the ACL on the primary switch to each interface where it is applied. This triggers proper synchronization to the secondary switch. The following is an example of proper synchronization: vlan 110 apply access-list ip list1 out vlan 120 apply access-list ip list1 out vlan 130 apply access-list ip list1 out.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 360748?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 360748): Symptom: BGP selects the wrong path, causing routing issues. Scenario: In some scenarios BGP incorrectly selects the path with the highest AS-Path. This causes incorrect paths to be propagated to the Routing Information Base (RIB) and Forwarding Information Base (FIB), potentially causing routing issues. Workaround: Lower the local preference of EVPN originated router. This causes the higher local preference route to be selected.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 359279?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 359279): Symptom: After a virtual machine is migrated (vMotion) from one data center to another in a static VXLAN environment, the VM cannot reach its gateway or external destinations, resulting in ping timeouts and connection failures. Scenario: In some scenarios, the switch can fail to program tunnel to VNI associations, leading to traffic loss. Workaround: Remove the affected VNI from the configuration on the switches in the source data center and then add the VNI to the configuration again.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 359666?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 359666): Symptom: With hpe-relay core dump legacy bootp client does not get IP. Scenario: When the DHCP relay daemon processes a legacy BOOTP client packet that lacks the option-53 field, it fails to properly validate the packet's structure before attempting to access this field, leading to a crash. This issue occurs exclusively when the smart-relay feature is enabled. Workaround: Disable the global DHCP smart-relay configuration if not required using no dhcp-smart-relay.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in Multicast Bug 342612?
original_answer: Multicast (Bug ID 342612): Symptom: When switching between IPTV channels, there is an intermittent delay of 2-6 seconds before the new video stream is displayed to the end user. Scenario: The system experiences a small delay in the program of a new multicast flow after receiving the IGMP report. During this time, critical initial video packets containing SPS (Sequence Parameter Set) and PPS (Picture Parameter Set) information may be dropped. These packets are essential for the decoder to properly display the H.264 video stream. Workaround: Configure static IGMP groups for the multicast streams using static igmp join <multicast-group-address> interface <decoder-port>. This ensures the hardware is pre-programmed with the multicast flows, eliminating the join latency.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in Multicast Bug 342612?
cleaned_answer: Multicast (Bug ID 342612): Symptom: When switching between IPTV channels, there is an intermittent delay of 2-6 seconds before the new video stream is displayed to the end user. During this time, critical initial video packets containing SPS (Sequence Parameter Set) and PPS (Picture Parameter Set) information may be dropped. These packets are essential for the decoder to properly display the H.264 video stream. Workaround: Configure static IGMP groups for the multicast streams using static igmp join <multicast-group-address> interface <decoder-port>. This ensures the hardware is pre-programmed with the multicast flows, eliminating the join latency.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what issue was resolved in REST Bug 353178?
original_answer: REST (Bug ID 353178): Symptom: When uploading firmware using the Web UI or REST API, the system displays the following error message despite the firmware being successfully transferred to the device: There was an error uploading new firmware. Firmware image is invalid. Please enter a valid firmware image. Scenario: When logging console is enabled in the switch configuration and a user attempts to upload firmware through the Web UI or REST API, the upload process is completed, but an HTTP 500 error is generated. Workaround: Until upgrading to the fixed version, use one of the following methods: 1. Disable console logging by removing logging console from the configuration. Then attempt the firmware upload using the Web UI or REST API again. 2. Use TFTP for firmware upload by uploading the firmware using TFTP or SFTP instead of the Web UI or REST API. Example command: copy tftp/sftp://[server-ip]/[firmware-file] primary vrf mgmt.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1020, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes, no traffic loss will be seen. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what caveat is documented for Certificates?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes, no traffic loss will be seen. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1030, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
cleaned_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes with no traffic loss. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration use more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to exceed the max of 256 temporarily. The scenario depends on the timing and ordering of route events during that period. If this max is hit, routes will be programmed as single and won't have protection until a new event converts them back to protected routes. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.

Scenario: In VSX deployments, multicast replication may fail during system boot or reload if the associated L3 interfaces for overlay VLANs are not fully initialized. This is more likely in configurations involving MCLAGs and delayed VSX synchronization, where multicast processing begins before the L3 interface is ready. Workaround: Toggle the pim-sparse configuration on the impacted interfaces.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1040, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes, no traffic loss will be seen. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.15.1060, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_answer: Logging (Bug ID 358847): Symptom: A VSF stack member switch unexpectedly reboots due to the ops-switchd process crashing with a signal: 6 error. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 371954?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 371954): Symptom: In an L2VPN network, when a DHCP client sends an IP request with the broadcast flag unset (unicast), duplicate DHCP client packets are observed at the DHCP server-one from each VTEP. As a result, the client does not receive an IP address. Scenario: To prevent duplicate DHCP client packets from each VTEP in an L2VPN topology, it is recommended to configure "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" on the switch. However, after saving the switch configuration and rebooting, the relay daemon does not correctly apply all configuration settings at boot time, even though the configuration persists in the database. This causes the duplicate packet issue to reappear. Workaround: To restore correct behavior, manually enable and then disable the L2VPN client configuration using "dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" followed by "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients".
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 371954?

As a result, the client does not receive an IP address. However, after saving the switch configuration and rebooting, the relay daemon does not correctly apply all configuration settings at boot time, even though the configuration persists in the database. This causes the duplicate packet issue to reappear. Workaround: To restore correct behavior, manually enable and then disable the L2VPN client configuration using 'dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients' followed by 'no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients'.

reason: The original answer contains unnecessary details that do not directly address the issue resolved by Bug 373232.
original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 373232?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 373232): Symptom: Multicast traffic loss may occur on interfaces with PIM-Sparse Mode (PIM-SM) enabled in overlay networks. Affected multicast flows fail to replicate across VXLAN tunnels, resulting in service disruption. Scenario: In VSX deployments, multicast replication may fail during system boot or reload if the associated L3 interfaces for overlay VLANs are not fully initialized. This is more likely in configurations involving MCLAGs and delayed VSX synchronization, where multicast processing begins before the L3 interface is ready. Workaround: Toggle the pim-sparse configuration on the impacted interfaces.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 410569?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 410569): Symptom: Interface Vxlan configuration is disabled on 9300 platform, for spine & DC-Optimized-Spine profiles from 10.15 onwards. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, what known issue is documented for OSPFv2 Bug 326021?
original_answer: OSPFv2 (Bug ID 326021): Symptom: Some protected route prefixes will be programmed as single routes, no traffic loss will be seen. Scenario: When the topology and route configuration uses more than 120 unique next-hop pairs for the route protection, a network event can cause the number of unique next-hop pairs to be higher than the max of 256 for a momentary period. The scenario of hitting this max on network events depends on the timing and ordering of the route events during that period. If the scenario hits the max allowed nexthop pairs, routes would be programed as single and will not have protection unless it gets a new event to convert it to a protected route. Workaround: Use protected nexthop groups of up to 120 instead of 256.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1010, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1010, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. As the implementation is software-based, the stop times for MACsec secure channel and secure associations are only updated when they are deleted and therefore never updated in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1010, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - The implementation uses a software-based approach to track start and stop times for secure channels and associations. Consequently, the stop times are only updated when the secure channel or association is deleted, and thus are not reflected in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

Scenario: During certain port flap events, STP may erroneously mark a port as Disabled/Blocked, even when the link should remain active. Workaround: Shut down all affected ports. Restart the hpe-pvstd process using the following commands: start-shell sudo bash ps aux | grep hpe-pvstd | grep -v grep systemctl restart hpe-pvstd ps aux | grep hpe-pvstd | grep -v grep exit exit

original_answer: EVPN (Bug ID 372373): Symptom: Traffic loss occurs for existing networks in a bundle when the VLAN-aware bundle is disabled or enabled for VLAN-ethernet-tag updates. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what caveat is documented for Certificates?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what issue was resolved in IGMP Bug 400918?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 400918): Symptom: The replication group of MCAST entry points to overlay-egress-obj, which does not exist in the hardware. The replication group points to the right net-vp. As a result, the VXLAN encapsulated packet containing the MCAST inner packet goes out with outer header SA and DST MAC as all zeros. Scenario: This issue occurs when the underlay nexthop changes towards the VTEP, which is connected to the client. The nexthop change can happen in the following cases: spine reboot, link flap, and interface MTU change.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1020, what issue was resolved in SNMP Bug 400261?
original_answer: SNMP (Bug ID 400261): Symptom: SNMP OID sysUpTimeInstance (1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0) may return an incorrect value when polled, leading to inaccurate uptime reporting in monitoring systems. Scenario: This issue occurs on switches running AOS-CX 10.16 when using SNMP to query sysUpTimeInstance. The reported value does not match the actual system uptime, causing discrepancies in monitoring tools. Workaround: Use the OID hrSystemUptime (1.3.6.1.2.1.25.1.1.0) instead of sysUpTimeInstance for accurate system uptime reporting.

original_answer: ARP (Bug ID 404262): Symptom: After switch reboot, keep alive neighbor may flap continuously and subsequent ARP resolution to random neighbors may fail. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. As the implementation is software-based, the stop times for MACsec secure channel and secure associations are only updated when they are deleted and therefore never updated in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1030, what issue was resolved in DHCP Server Bug 348727?
original_answer: DHCP Server (Bug ID 348727): Symptom: DHCP-Server configuration status becomes stuck in pending state after configuration changes are pushed from Aruba Central, preventing proper DHCP service operation. Scenario: This issue occurs when administrators make configuration changes using Aruba Central, either through Central template groups or the UI group multi-edit option. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what issue was resolved in MAC Tables Bug 415095?
original_answer: MAC Tables (Bug ID 415095): Symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what known issue is documented for PBR Bug 407418?
original_answer: PBR (Bug ID 407418): Symptom: Classifier Policy is not enforced for traffic coming from an ISL link. Scenario: For a topology with VSX+ VRRP, if the policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting classifier policy enforced. Even though the policy action is PBR redirect, those packets are not getting redirected. Workaround: Two workarounds are possible to prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL. 1. Use active gateway to give better active active redundancy. Packets reaching either VSX device process the packet destined to active gateway. 2. Turn off the interface connection from VRRP secondary device to down switch. This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in BGP Bug 372373?
original_answer: BGP (Bug ID 372373): Symptom: Ethernet-tag mappings are permitted only when the operational state of the EVPN VLAN-aware bundle is disabled. Scenario: In previous versions of AOS-CX, modifications to VLAN: Ethernet-tag mappings were permitted only when the operational state of the EVPN VLAN-aware bundle was disabled. In AOS-CX 10.17, this restriction has been relaxed-modifications are now allowed regardless of the operational state, provided that the resulting effective Ethernet tag does not conflict with any existing VLAN tag assignment. This ensures configuration flexibility while maintaining tag uniqueness and preventing forwarding conflicts.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in Classifier Bug 394785?
original_answer: Classifier (Bug ID 394785): Symptom: A Cfg_version being set to 0 results in a policy always having the in-progress message attached to it, without any changes being made to hardware resources. This will not result in a crash. The system has a low chance of getting corrupted internal data for classifier lists (ACLs, policies, etc) over time. Once this data gets corrupted, any changes to a classifier list will cause the system to crash. Scenario: Setting the cfg_version on any classifier list to 0 results in the list being perpetually stuck "in-progress". There is no specific configuration or deployment model that can cause corrupted internal data for classifier lists; this can happen randomly. Workaround: Setting cfg_version to any valid schema value other than 0 and not the current in_progress_version value will allow configurations to be processed.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in Classifier Bug 394785?
cleaned_answer: Classifier (Bug ID 394785): Symptom: Setting cfg_version to 0 results in a policy always having the in-progress message attached to it without any changes being made to hardware resources. This will not result in a crash but may lead to corrupted internal data for classifier lists over time, causing crashes upon any changes to classifier lists. Workaround: Set cfg_version to any valid schema value other than 0 and not the current in_progress_version value.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in Classifier Bug 335358?
original_answer: Classifier (Bug ID 335358): Symptom: IP packets containing options headers bypass routed-in security policies configured with drop actions, resulting in policy enforcement failure. Scenario: When security policies are applied in the routed-in direction with drop actions configured, IP packets containing options headers are being processed by the policy engine but are circumventing the intended policy enforcement, causing these packets to be incorrectly forwarded through the system instead of being dropped as intended. Resolution: This issue has been resolved in the current implementation. Note that alternative TCAM configurations were not affected by this behavioral anomaly.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.0001, what issue was resolved in VXLAN Bug 410569?
original_answer: VXLAN (Bug ID 410569): Symptom: Interface Vxlan configuration is disabled on 9300 platform, for spine & DC-Optimized-Spine profiles from 10.15 onwards. Scenario: If Spine profile with VxLAN configs is being used before 10.15 release, then they must change the profile to Leaf before upgrading to 10.15 or higher CX versions to avoid config loss / traffic outage. Workaround: Change to Leaf profile and perform switch reboot - before upgrading to 10.15 or higher CX versions.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1001, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. As the implementation is software-based, the stop times for MACsec secure channel and secure associations are only updated when they are deleted and therefore never updated in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.
cleaned_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - The implementation uses a software-based approach to track start and stop times for secure channels and associations. Consequently, the stop times are only updated when the secure channel or association is deleted, and thus are not reflected in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1001, what issue was resolved in Central connectivity Bug 392076?
original_answer: Central connectivity (Bug ID 392076): Symptom: Switch is unable to connect to Central. Scenario: This issue may occur when attempting to onboard switches that are connected to Central. The process connecting to YANG is creating more sockets than expected, leading to overload of the yang-resolverd server and subsequent disconnections. Workaround: Restart yang-resolverd daemon to reestablish the operation of the daemon. Restart hpe-restd daemon to make a clean connection to YANG after restarting the yang-resolverd daemon.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: MACsec - MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what issue was resolved in MAC Tables Bug 415095?
original_answer: MAC Tables (Bug ID 415095): Symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. Scenario: This issue can occur after resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move from one VSX device to the other, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Using the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

Scenario: This issue can occur after resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move between the two VSX devices, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Use the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1010, what known issue is documented for PBR Bug 407418?
original_answer: PBR (Bug ID 407418): Symptom: Classifier Policy is not enforced for traffic coming from an ISL link. Scenario: For a topology with VSX+ VRRP, if the policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting classifier policy enforced. Even though the policy action is PBR redirect, those packets are not getting redirected. Workaround: Two workarounds are possible to prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL. 1. Use active gateway to give better active active redundancy. Packets reaching either VSX device process the packet destined to active gateway. 2. Turn off the interface connection from VRRP secondary device to down switch. This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in Classifier Bug 366902?
original_answer: Classifier (Bug ID 366902): Symptom: Policy hitcounts commands using the vrf specifier do not include policies applied to L3 VNIs associated with the specified VRF. Scenario: This issue may occur when configuring an L3 VNI with a VRF, applying a policy to it, and sending traffic to increment hitcounts. Attempting to show policy hitcounts for the VRF fails and a report that no policy is applied to the VRF. Attempting to clear hitcounts for the VRF has no effect. Workaround: When showing or clearing hitcounts for all applications associated with the VRF, use a separate command to show or clear hitcounts for each L3 VNI.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in DHCP Relay Bug 423594?
original_answer: DHCP Relay (Bug ID 423594): Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

original_answer: PKI (Bug ID 428981): Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

original_question: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what issue was resolved in UDP Forwarder Bug 418074?
original_answer: UDP Forwarder (Bug ID 418074): Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.
